topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink bled across the paper, a stark, undeniable indictment of my efforts. A C- in Advanced Calculus. It was a disaster. I had stayed up until 3 AM, wrestling with those differential equations, sacrificing sleep and social time for this. Now, staring at the grade, a hot, prickly feeling crawled up my neck. I picked up my pen, the plastic feeling slick and foreign in my hand, and dragged it across the margin, making a jagged, ugly line through the word 'satisfactory.' Why did they do this? It felt like a deliberate slight, a dismissal of everything I’d poured into this subject. I shoved the paper into my backpack, the sound of the zipper a dull, defeated rasp. I just wanted to be able to throw it away, to erase the evidence that I hadn't been good enough.

[story 2]

My stomach clenched into a tight knot as I read the feedback. 'Insufficient depth in application.' Insufficient depth. It was so vague, so dismissive. I traced the words with my eyes, the fluorescent lights of the library buzzing overhead, amplifying the hollowness in my chest. I slammed the textbook shut, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet study carrel. I leaned back in my chair, letting my shoulders slump, a heavy weight settling onto my spine. I picked up my phone, scrolling through social media, the bright, careless updates of others mocking the slow, grinding disappointment inside me. I muttered under my breath, a low, tight sound, "Of course." It was the simplest thing to say, yet it tasted like ash.

[story 3]

The number sat there, glaring. 68%. I stared at it until the edges of the paper seemed to blur. A slow, cold wave washed over me, chilling the heat rising in my cheeks. I picked up my pencil, but instead of writing, I tapped it rhythmically against the desk, a frantic, uneven beat. Every minute of the past few weeks—the late nights fueled by lukewarm coffee, the meticulous note-taking, the sheer mental strain—it all seemed to dissolve into this single, inadequate score. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping backward, and walked toward the window. The city lights below looked indifferent, glittering without concern for the struggle I’d just endured. I clenched my jaw, refusing to let the feeling settle into anything constructive.
